posts.name: Noah C @ P U R 5 0
posts.cooked:
Hi everyone-

I’m about to turn 40 and I plan to mark the occasion by cooking dinner and opening a 1983 Chateau Margaux and 1983 Chateau Suduiraut. I’d love any advice on serving (decant? how long? etc) and food pairings.

I’d love to hear what you think of my plan:
Margaux- Was going to decant off sediment just before dinner and serve, letting the wine open over the course of dinner. Steak is the obvious pairing but I thought the Margaux would be a on the subtle/gentle side, so was planning on Filet Mignon, maybe with a mushroom cream sauce.
Suduiraut- Was planning to PnP. I was thinking cheese rather than pair with a sweet dessert, not sure whether before or after dinner. No idea how to source foie gras.

I’m a bit out of my depth here, so would love any advice.

Thanks!
Noah

posts.name: Chuck Miller
posts.cooked: If the Suduiraut is a 750ml, then you can serve it with foie gras before the main course AND with cheese after the main course.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: if it’s a well-stored bottle and you get a good, firm cork, then 30+ minutes’ decant will only do the 1983 Margaux favors

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked: I had the 83 suduiraut last year from 375, and it had shed much of its youthful sweetness, and read as off-dry (say, kabinett-like). It was lovely and better than the ‘83 Climens I had a bit earlier. We drank it on its own after dinner, but I think it would have paired nicely with a simple stone fruit and cream dessert - poached apricots with creme anglaise or whipped cream, for example. Grilled peaches might be nice too.

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked:
I think you’re bang-on with the Margaux and the Filet. The sauce sounds especially great.

I’ve really rarely found any dessert and Sauternes pairings to be pleasing. So often the sweetness of the dessert overwhelms the palate and leaves a sour metallic taste behind when you sip the Sauternes. Especially with an aged example where the perceived sugar have receded a tad. The fruit and cream suggestion above could be a nice exception. Another I have occasionally enjoyed is Japanese Cheesecake without any toppings. Light, fluffy, cheesy, and not terribly sweet, generally speaking.

I really enjoy Sauternes before a meal with hors d’oeuvres. Foie Gras is the strongest choice in my view. Many smaller boutique and specialty grocers near me sell the little jars or tins, often from Quebec or France. Often they’ll be labeled “foie gras entier” or “bloc de foie gras”. I prefer these to “mousse de foie gras” which is more whipped and less solid and meaty I find. Take it out of the fridge 20 or 30 minutes before eating, spread with a knife over brioche or a fresh country sourdough, and top with a little caramelized onion and you’re in heaven. I’d forget about trying to sear the foie gras. Too much technique involved and variability. If you get a piece with veins in it, it can really kill the experience. I’m also aware of some who like Roquefort and Sauternes which I think can be successful, but a lot of what gets imported can have a bit of an off taste/aroma that I think can kill the experience. Either way, in my view, as long a you leave a little time, say 30 to 60 minutes, from when you have the Sauternes until you get into the steak and Margaux your palate has plenty of time to recover from the sugar. I’d also consider having a cheese or two on hand for when you’ve inevitably finished the steak but still have have half the bottle of wine in the decanter.
posts.display_username: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Chris_V:

I think you’re bang-on with the Margaux and the Filet. The sauce sounds especially great.

I’ve really rarely found any dessert and Sauternes pairings to be pleasing. So often the sweetness of the dessert overwhelms the palate and leaves a sour metallic taste behind when you sip the Sauternes. Especially with an aged example where the perceived sugar have receded a tad. The fruit and cream suggestion above could be a nice exception. Another I have occasionally enjoyed is Japanese Cheesecake without any toppings. Light, fluffy, cheesy, and not terribly sweet, generally speaking.

I agree that filet with mushroom sauce and the Margaux sound great.

And Chris is so right about late harvest wines. Dessert wines are so often paired with conventional very sweet desserts and they compete. It’s a disaster. The Italians have a knack for good, not-terribly-sweet desserts. You might consider that. I’ve found that nut cakes can work well with late harvest wines, though I usually cut the sugar in the recipe by a third or half. I could imagine an unsweetened puff pastry glazed with honey could work, too. Or biscotti. They are made to dunk in vin santo, after all.

With those kinds of desserts, the wine plays off the food rather than wrestles with it for supremacy.

reply_to_user.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
Noah_C:

I’d love to hear what you think of my plan:
Margaux- Was going to decant off sediment just before dinner and serve, letting the wine open over the course of dinner.

William_Kelley:

if it’s a well-stored bottle and you get a good, firm cork, then 30+ minutes’ decant will only do the 1983 Margaux favors

It’s always difficult to give advice for old/mature bottles as they can be/are so different depending on storage/cork. The Margaux: I had it twice in the past few 2.5 years and both were really well stored bottles. They needed even more than the 30 minutes to fully blossom. I would open it earlier (at least tow hours before serving) and depending on how the wine is showing, I would decant it not at all or up to the full two hours.

Noah_C:

Suduiraut- Was planning to PnP. I was thinking cheese rather than pair with a sweet dessert, not sure whether before or after dinner. No idea how to source foie gras.

I haven’t had the Suduiraut 1983 recently, but in my experience, (good!) Sauternes almost always need quite some air and time to fully shine. I don’t think that you can destroy the Suduiraut, even from half bottle, when opening it a bit earlier and see for yourself if it needs some time in the decanter or not.

posts.name: Tom R W  \/\/@rren
posts.cooked:
Filet and mushrooms should be divine!
Sounds like plenty of good advice about the wines already.

I order foie from Au Bon Canard or D’artagnan when needed. ABC is a MN farm and supplies many famous restaurants.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: Beware of the 1983 Margaux. Very, very hit and miss; when perfect it is gorgeous, but there seem to be a lot of bad bottles floating around. I would have a back up just in case.

posts.name: Gary Rust
posts.cooked: Agree that the '83 Margaux is a minefield and backup is warranted. If it is a good bottle thought it’s not a light Bordeaux, but muscular still.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Noah_C:

I’m about to turn 40 and I plan to mark the occasion by cooking dinner and opening a 1983 Chateau Margaux and 1983 Chateau Suduiraut. I’d love any advice on serving (decant? how long? etc) and food pairings

Happy early Bday!

Do not over decant your 83 Margaux. You have more to lose than gain by too much air. 20-30 minutes is ample and from there, the wine will improve in the glass. Else, stand the bottle up for a few days in advance so the sediment drops. For pairing, pick your favorite, meat or fowl course that makes you happy and you will be sitting pretty.

On the Suduiraut, I find it’s best paired with cheese. Sweet on sweet is cloying and makes it hard to enjoy either the wine, or the dessert.

posts.name: Bill Spohn
posts.cooked: I agree about Sauternes - I am doing a dinner in a couple of months that will showcase three different vintages of a particular Sauternes with seared foie gras and coarse salt and nothing else but a couple of slices of toasted brioche. The three will be a decade or so apart from each other so it should be interesting.

posts.name: M a r k K a p l a n
posts.cooked: I differ slightly with this comment. It is undeniably true for bottles purchased in the secondary marketplace, now 40 years past vintage, but untrue for bottles purchased around the time of release from trusted importers/retailers. Properly stored bottles continue to shine; I have one standing up for near future consumption.
posts.display_username: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.name: Johan Berglund / The Wine Independent
posts.cooked:
Having opened quite a few 83s the last year as one of my best friends is of the same vintage I would suggest a longer decant than you might think.

Other than the superstars Margaux and Palmer a couple of times each also the less talked about Gloria, Chasse-Spleen, Labégorce-Zédé and Taillefer. Always at 60-120 minutes in decanter and never did a bottle not improve with air.

The QPR of the lot is definitely Labégorce-Zédé which you will find standing up really well in a blind competition of 83s.

Biggest surprise is Taillefer which is just lovely.

I agree with previous posters on NEVER pairing Sauternes with dessert. Sauternes is magical and desert kills it. The 1983 Yquem is also a just brilliant wine should you come across it. Am sure the Suduiraut will be great also.

Cheers

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Has anyone ever wondered if Sauternes before dinner affects your palate for dry Bordeaux at dinner?

I had d’Yquem with foie gras before a First Growth dinner at Marche Moderne once. A few of us wondered after if it detracted some from the Bordeaux.

I am not asserting that point of view, but just wondering if anyone else has ever questioned that sequence.

posts.name: Claus Jeppesen
posts.cooked:
1983 was stellar in Margaux
If you will invite guests to your b day 2 wines will certainly not be enough
Mosel Nahe and Rheingau. You can still find gems fron this great German vintage

posts.name: M a r k K a p l a n
posts.cooked: One must make sure there is a good food and neutral beverage buffer between sweet wines and dry wines, or the dry wines invariably suffer. A bread and butter course or something like, followed by a glass of brut Champagne or beer, works wonders.
posts.display_username: M a r k K a p l a n

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Chris_Seiber:

Has anyone ever wondered if Sauternes before dinner affects your palate for dry Bordeaux at dinner?

Today, at d’Yquem and at other estates in Sauternes, meals begin with a sweet wine as an aperitif, paired with spicy foods or simple, salty dishes before moving to Champagne, white or red.

In fact, Chateau d’Yquem has started a program called Lighthouse, where their wine is available by the glass with suggested pairings for apéritifs’, during or after dinner.

posts.name: Bill Johnson
posts.cooked:
I would open the Margaux with a Durand 2-4 hours before dinner. If the bouquet is fantastic, as it might be, I would put in a fresh cork and revisit the wine about half an hour before dinner to smell and take a small taste and decide whether to leave the cork out or not. If the wine is unpleasant or dull upon opening, I would leave it open. It might come alive. In any case, I would not decant the wine.

IMO, younger Sauternes are generally very good with some desserts; for example, with an apple tart. Older Sauternes are best by themselves or with a mild cheese and/or salted nuts.
